text: Daniel Dennett argues that the self is not a fixed entity residing in the brain but a "center of narrative gravity" — a fictional point around which stories about our experience cohere. Just as a physical center of gravity is a useful abstraction rather than a thing you can touch, the narrative self is a useful fiction the brain constructs to make sense of disparate experiences. The danger, Dennett suggests, is mistaking this abstraction for a real agent. When we over-identify with our narrative, we defend it compulsively, attending only to information that confirms the story and filtering out everything that challenges it.
question: According to Dennett, what does over-identifying with the narrative self cause us to do?

options.text: Develop a more coherent sense of personal identity over time
Lose access to unconscious memories stored in the brain
Attend only to information that confirms our story, filtering out challenges
Confuse the physical brain with abstract cognitive processes
